coredata.dc:title: Two-photon ratiometric carbon dot-based probe for real-time intracellular pH monitoring in 3D environment
coredata.dc:creator: Lesani, Pooria
Singh, Gurvinder
Lu, Zufu
Mirkhalaf, Mohammad
New, Elizabeth J.
Zreiqat, Hala
coredata.dc:description: Designing a two-photon ratiometric pH-sensitive nanoprobe for real time monitoring of intracellular pH in biological environment is of great importance for better understanding the pathogenesis of diseases and the design of intracellular drug delivery-based system. However, the development of such probe remains a challenge. Here, we report for the first time the design and development of the two-photon ratiometric carbon dot-based nanoprobe and demonstrate its capability to monitor and quantify the intracellular cytoplasmic pH value, in real time. The nanoprobe comprised of a fluorescent carbon dot functionalized with a pH-responsive fluorescein dye (FACD). With increasing pH, FACD exhibits a clear ratiometric change in the emission intensity ratio, with sensitivity across a wide pH range in both extracellular and intracellular compartments. FACD is non-toxic to adipose stem cells in cell imaging experimental conditions and exhibits remarkable thermal stability, photostability, and two-photon near-infrared excitation capability. Using real-time dual-channel two-photon confocal microscopy we demonstrate the great potential of FACD as an efficient nanoprobe with high accuracy for the intracellular sensing of pH in living adipose stem cells seeded on either cell-culture dishes or on a 3D printed bioactive scaffold.
coredata.dcterms:subject: carbon dot
ratiometric pH nanoprobe
two-photon microscopy
real-time intracellular pH sensing
3D environment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum yield and Two-Photon absorption Cross-Section measurements
Fluorescence responses of FACD to pH and potential interference molecules
Intracellular pH calibration and Real-Time monitoring of intracellular cytoplasmic pH using FACD
Scaffold 3D printing and cell seeding for cytoplasmic pH sensing in live cells
Development of a two-photon ratiometric ultrasensitive pH-responsive carbon dot-based nanoprobe
Real-time monitoring of intracellular pH in live cells
Real-time monitoring of intracellular pH in live cells seeded on 3D environment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (purple), fluorescence excitation (green; λem = 450 nm), fluorescence emission (pink; λex = 360 nm) spectra; (B) fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelength (320–420 nm); (C) FT-IR spectra; (D) XPS survey spectra of 30–100 kDa-CD. EX denotes the excitation wavelength.

(A) Absorption (green) and fluorescence emission (pink; λex = 350 nm) spectra of FACD; (B) TEM image of FACD, inset is the particle size distribution. Scale bar represents 20 nm; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of FACD under single-photon (λex = 350 nm) in HEPES buffer with varying pH; (D) Calibration curve of IFA / ICD versus pH value obtained from Fig. 2C.

(A) Thermal stability of FACD at different temperatures and pH values; (B) Photostability of FACD at different pH values under continuous irradiation with UV light (500 W); (C) Response of FACD (800 µg/mL) to various metal ions (250 µM) and biological molecules (arginine (Arg), glucose (Gluc), cysteine (Cys), dopamine (DA), glutamic acid (Glut), glycine (Gly), H2O2) at concentrations of 1 mM; (D) Cell viability of ASCs incubated with FACD at increasing concentration for 7 days (mean ± SD, n = 3). Cells incubated with only media as control.

Ratiometric intracellular calibration of pH in live ASCs using FACD. (A) Two-photon confocal microscopy images of FACD in ASCs at pH values of (a-c) 5.0, (d-f) 6.0, (g-i) 6.5, (j-l) 7.0, (m-o) 7.5. ASCs incubated with 800 µg/mL FACD were respectively treated with high K+ buffer containing 10 µM nigericin with different pH values (5.0–7.5) at 37℃ for 40 min. The CD and FA fluorescence emission were collected at 385–495 nm and 500–650 nm, respectively, with single excitation at 750 nm through a 63 × 1.4NA objective (scale: 30 µm). (B) Quantification of intracellular fluorescence intensity ratios (IFA / ICD) with varying cytoplasmic pH values. Inset: Linear plotting of IFA / ICD versus pH in the range of 5.0 to 7.5, obtained from Fig. 4A. Ratio bar: 0–6.

(A) Real-time dual-channel two-photon confocal microscopy of ASCs incubated with FACD and treated with high K+ buffer containing 10 µM nigericin with pH value of 5 and imaged at 5 min intervals for 40 min (two-photon excitation wavelength: 750 nm, Scale bar: 30 µm). (B) Scatter plot of intracellular fluorescence intensity ratio (IFA / ICD) in ASCs over time.

(A) Schematic of the microstructure of cylindrical Baghdadite scaffold (diameter: 6 mm) with a cubic internal architecture with 50% porosity and 300 µm pore size. (B) Overlay of DIC and two-photon fluorescence images and (C-O) two-photon excited Z-stack images of the Baghdadite scaffold seeded with ASCs after incubation with FACD (800 μg/mL) for 24 h (from 0 to 120 μm; Δz = 10 μm, Scale: 100 μm).

Real-time dual-channel two-photon confocal microscopy of ASCs, seeded on the Baghdadite scaffold, incubated with FACD (800 µg/mL, 24 h), and treated with high K+ buffer containing 10 µM nigericin with pH value of 5 and imaged for 80 min (excitation wavelength: 750 nm, Scale bar: 30 µm). (B) Scatter plot of intracellular fluorescence intensity ratio (IFA / ICD) in ASCs versus time.

head.ce:title: Two-photon ratiometric carbon dot-based probe for real-time intracellular pH monitoring in 3D environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tissue Engineering & Biomaterials Research Unit, School of Biomedical Engineering, the University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tissue Engineering & Biomaterials Research Unit, School of Biomedical Engineering, the University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ARC Training Centre for Innovative BioEngineering, The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ARC Training Centre for Innovative BioEngineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ARC Training Centre for Innovative BioEngineering, The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney Nano Institute (Sydney Nano), The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of Sydney Nano Institute (Sydney Nano), The University of Sydney

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The University of Sydney Nano Institute (Sydney Nano), The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, The University of Sydney, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing a two-photon ratiometric pH-sensitive nanoprobe for real time monitoring of intracellular pH in biological environment is of great importance for better understanding the pathogenesis of diseases and the design of intracellular drug delivery-based system. However, the development of such probe remains a challenge. Here, we report for the first time the design and development of the two-photon ratiometric carbon dot-based nanoprobe and demonstrate its capability to monitor and quantify the intracellular cytoplasmic pH value, in real time. The nanoprobe comprised of a fluorescent carbon dot functionalized with a pH-responsive fluorescein dye (FACD). With increasing pH, FACD exhibits a clear ratiometric change in the emission intensity ratio, with sensitivity across a wide pH range in both extracellular and intracellular compartments. FACD is non-toxic to adipose stem cells in cell imaging experimental conditions and exhibits remarkable thermal stability, photostability, and two-photon near-infrared excitation capability. Using real-time dual-channel two-photon confocal microscopy we demonstrate the great potential of FACD as an efficient nanoprobe with high accuracy for the intracellular sensing of pH in living adipose stem cells seeded on either cell-culture dishes or on a 3D printed bioactive scaffold.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular pH (pHi) is a key to regulating many processes in cellular physiology such as cell apoptosis, proliferation and differentiation, cell membrane polarity and ion transport, muscular contraction, cytoskeletal interactions, and cellular signaling [1–3]. Dramatic fluctuations in pHi can cause inappropriate cell function, growth, and division, and lead to serious diseases including rheumatoid arthritis [4], ischemic/reperfusion injury [5], cancers [3], Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease [5]. This strong interdependence between cellular functionality and cytoplasmic pH values has led to the recent focus of research efforts on the development of a sensitive and cost-effective technique for long-term and real-time monitoring of pHi [6,7].

Numerous analytical and quantification techniques have been developed for pHi sensing. In particular, fluorescence-based optical sensing is widely used because of its excellent spatial and temporal resolution, real-time monitoring, non-invasiveness, simple instrumentation, and ease of sample preparation [8]. The main focus of previous studies has been on developing intensity-based nanoprobes with single-photon excitation capability [3,9]. The output of intensity-based nanoprobes can be significantly influenced by the stability of the excitation source, photobleaching of the dyes and other background effects which may result in providing inaccurate measured results [10,11]. Recently, ratiometric nanoprobes have been explored as alternatives to intensity-based nanoprobes for various biosensing applications [12]. In ratiometric probes, the analyte induces a change in the spectral form and the ratio of two independent emission peaks reports on analyte concentration, but not probe accumulation [13], offering improved sensitivity. Furthermore, ratiometric nanoprobes are not susceptible to interferences from background effects [14].

On the other hand, two-photon excitable probes with the ability to simultaneously absorb the energy of two near-infrared photons offer several advantages, such as minimal interference from background species, lower risk of photodamage to cells and tissue, longer observation times, and higher penetration depth [8,15]. A limited number of two-photon pH-sensitive nanoprobes have been reported to date, but their application has been limited by insufficient photostability and complex design and synthesis procedures [10,15]. To close the existing gap in previous studies, it is crucial to develop the next generation of fluorescence nanoprobe with both ratiometric and two-photon excitable properties for real-time determination of intracellular cytoplasmic pH in living cells.

In this study, we aimed to develop a novel ultrasensitive carbon dot (CD) based two-photon ratiometric fluorescent pH-sensitive nanoprobe. In recent years, CDs have been utilized for various biological applications because of their unique size-dependent optical properties, superior photostability, ease in surface functionalization, and good biocompatibility [8,16,17]. First, the novel synthesized CD was functionalized with fluoresceinamine as a secondary responsive fluorophore, to develop a ratiometric nanoprobe (FACD). The developed FACD demonstrates superior photophysical and biological properties including single-photon and near-infrared two-photon excitation capability, single-wavelength excitation, good thermal stability and photostability, excellent ratiometric fluorescence sensitivity, specificity, and reversibility to pH fluctuation, biocompatibility, and good cellular internalization. We then investigated the ability of FACD for accurate long-term and real-time monitoring of intracellular cytoplasmic pH fluctuations modulated by nigericin in both 2D and 3D environments.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. L-Ascorbic acid, L-arginine, ethanol, acetonitrile, N-ethyl-N′-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), fluoresceinamine-isomer I (FA), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide, 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), MgCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, AlCl3, CuCl2, CoCl2, Ni(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Ag(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, MgSO4, D-(+)-Glucose, sodium phosphate monobasic and lead acetate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Australia). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), TrypsinLE Express and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Gibco Life Technologies (USA). Fetal calf serum (FCS), penicillin and streptomycin were purchased from ThermoFisher Scientific (USA). Primary Human Dermal Fibroblasts (PHDF) from foreskin were kindly donated by Prof. Rebecca Mason’s group at the University of Sydney. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium) (MTS) was purchased from Progema (USA). Deionized water, herein referred to as water, was used throughout.

CD were prepared via one-step hydrothermal method, adapted from our previous work [8]. Ascorbic acid (1.0 g) and L-arginine (1.5 g) were mixed in deionized (DI) water in a Teflon-lined reactor chambers with a 50% filling ratio and heated at 180℃ for 8 h in a furnace, and a dark brown solution was obtained. The solution was first passed through a 0.22 µm cellulose acetate filter, and then CD were separated by size by centrifugation through dialysis tubes with different molecular weight cut-offs (MWCO) to obtain CD with particle size distribution in the range of 30–100 kDa (30–100 kDa-CD). The resulting solutions were dried at 60℃ using a rotary evaporator and kept refrigerated at 4 °C before further use.

30–100 kDa-CD (1 mg/mL) were mixed with 13.7 mg of EDC.HCl and stirred for 5 min under nitrogen (N2) atmosphere. Next, 2.2 mg of DMAP was added to the reaction mixture and stirred for 1 h under N2 atmosphere. 6.3 mg fluoresceinamine (FA) dissolved in 1 mL DMSO was added to the reaction mixture and the reaction mixture was left to stir for 48 h in the dark. The resulting solution was purified by dialyzing against DI water for another 72 h in the dark. The product was dried at 60 °C using a rotary evaporator and kept for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by JEOL JEM-2100, operating at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using a Kratos Axis Nova spectrometer (Kratos Analytical, UK), equipped with a monochromatized aluminium X-ray source (Alkα 1486.6 eV) operating at 10 mA and 15 kV (150 kW). Survey and high-resolution spectra were acquired at detector pass energies of 160 and 20 eV, respectively. The XPS data were analyzed with Thermo Avantage processing software (v5. 9902). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed using a CLARIOstar and FLUOstar Omega (BMG LABTECH, USA), and Varioskan Lux (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Singapore) spectrometer. A Leica SP5 II multiphoton confocal microscope (USA) equipped with femtosecond pulsed infrared laser and benchtop incubator (5%, 37℃) was used for investigating the two-photon absorption, emission behavior of synthesized CD as well as live imaging of adipose stem cells (ASCs, purchased from Invitrogen) seeded on both glass bottom dish and 3D printed scaffold.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and Two-Photon absorption Cross-Section measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Quantum yield (QY) was calculated by plotting the absorbance values and integrated fluorescence intensities with FITC as the standard (QYR of 95%) and samples at the same wavelength.[18] The gradients of the linear fits of these lines were input into the following formula:
QY = Q
Y R
m
m R
n 2
n R 2

Where the subscript “R” refers to the reference FITC, whilst no subscript refers to the CD sample; “m” is the gradient of the linear fit of absorbance against integrated fluorescence intensity; “n” is the refractive index of the solvent which for FITC was ethanol and for carbon dots in water.

The two-photon fluorescence intensity of a fluorophore depends on its emission quantum yield (QY) and two-photon absorption cross-section (σ2p). By using fluorescein (FL) as the reference sample (σFL = 37 GM at 780 nm) [4], the two-photon absorption cross-section (TPA) of 30–100 KDa-CD (σCD) was calculated at 780 nm excitation wavelength. We measured the two-photon emission intensities of 30–100 KDa-CD (FCD) and fluorescein (FFL) at known concentrations (CCD and CFL) and the TPA of our probe was estimated as follows [9,19,20]:

σ CD = σ FL
F
CD
F
FL
Q Y
FL
Q Y
CD
C
FL
C
CD

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of FACD to pH and potential interference molecules
ce:section.ce:para: A series of standard pH buffers was prepared with 10 mM NaH2PO4 being adjusted by NaOH or HCl. Next, FACD solution at the concentration of 800 µg/mL with buffer solutions at varied pH values was prepared and transferred to a 96-well plate (200 µL) for fluorescence spectra measurement at single- and two-photon excitation at 350 and 750 nm, respectively. Finally, the maximum fluorescence emission of CD and FA were recorded and the fluorescence ratios (IFA / ICD) were calculated at different pH values. Fluorescence reversibility of FACD with pH was examined by varying the pH value between 4 and 10 for 7 cycle using diluted solution of HCl or NaOH. The pH value of the FACD solution was measured by pH-meter and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after each pH adjustment.

For specificity studies of FACD in the presence of metal ions and biological molecules, fluorescence detection was performed in 10 mM PBS buffer using 96-well plates. Each well was loaded with solution of FACD (800 µg/mL) and metal ions (250 µM) including Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ or biological molecules (1 mM) including arginine (Arg), glucose (Gluc), cysteine (Cys), dopamine (DA), glutamic acid (Glut), glycine (Gly), H2O2.

To determine thermal stability of FACD, an 800 µg/mL FACD dispersed in DI water was adjusted to different pH values (4–10) and aliquots transferred to a 96 well-plate (200 µL). Next, the temperature of the samples was accurately varied in the range of 25-37℃, using a Varioskan Lux (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Singapore) spectrometer equipped with a built-in incubator for temperature control, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded with λex = 350 nm. To examine the photostability of the nanoprobe, we then exposed the FACD solutions at different pH values to continuous UV light (500 W) irradiation for 3600 s at 25℃ and the fluorescence spectra were recorded with λex = 350 nm.

ASCs (Invitrogen) were propagated until passage 4 according to the manufacturer's instructions in MesenPRO RSTM Basal Medium (Invitrogen) supplemented with 2 mM l-glutamine and MesenPRO RS Growth Supplement (Invitrogen). ASCs at passage 4 were cultured in ventilated T-175 tissue culture flasks in complete medium containing α-MEM, supplemented with 10% (v/v) FCS, 30 mg/mL penicillin and 100 mg/mL streptomycin, herein referred to as supplemented DMEM. ASCs were incubated in supplemented DMEM at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 and the medium refreshed every 3 days until 80–90% confluence was reached.

A tetrazolium compound [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium, inner salt; MTS]-based cell viability assay was performed on ASCs that were incubated with increasing concentrations of FACD (100–800 µg/mL). ASCs were seeded at a density of 10,000 cells/well and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 for 24 h. Following this, stock solutions with FACD concentrations of 800 µg/mL in supplemented α-MEM were made. The stock solutions were sterilized by filtering with 0.22 µm cellulose acetate filters prior to incubation with cells. Stock solutions were further used to make solutions with FACD concentrations of 100, 200, 400, and 800 µg/mL. After 24 h of initial cell seeding, medium was removed from all well plates and replaced with 200 μL of either supplemented α-MEM as a control or FACD solutions of different concentrations. ASCs viability was assessed using CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Reagent (Promega) according to manufacturer’s instructions. Briefly, 100 µL of MTS solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C under 5% CO2. The absorbance of the MTS solution was then measured at 490 nm using Multiskan Sky Microplate Spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The cell viability (%) of wells was calculated relative to the control averages as per the formula:
Cellviability =
[ A ]
[ A ]
control
× 100

Where [A] is the average absorbance of the wells from days 1, 4 and 7 and [A]control is the average absorbance of the control wells from the day 1, 4, and 7 plates. The mean and standard error of the mean (SEM) of these cell viability percentages were calculated in Origin® 2018 and SEM plotted on column graphs as error bars.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular pH calibration and Real-Time monitoring of intracellular cytoplasmic pH using FACD
ce:section.ce:para: According to the procedure mentioned above, the ASCs cells were incubated with FACD (800 µg/mL) for 24 h at 37℃ and washed three times with PBS buffer after the culture medium was removed. The treated cells were then incubated with high K+ buffer containing 30 mM NaCl, 120 mM KCl, 1 mM CaCl2, 0.5 mM MgSO4, 1 mM NaH2PO4, 5 mM glucose, 20 mM HEPES, 15 µM nigericin with various pH values in the range of 5–7.5 at 37℃ for 40 min.

To further demonstrate the applicability of developed nanoprobe for long-term and real-time measurement of intracellular pH variations, we first incubated the ASCs with FACD (based on the procedure mentioned above). The resultant cells were then incubated with high K+ buffer with pH 5 to modulate the intracellular pH in ASCs [11]. The treated ASCs were immediately observed by two-photon confocal fluorescence microscope equipped with benchtop incubator (Leica TCS SP5 II) at 37℃ with 5% CO2 for 40 min at a time interval of 5 min.

Two-photon intracellular fluorescence images, for both pHi calibration and long-term and real-time monitoring of cytoplasmic pH in live cells, were captured using a Leica TCS SP5 II two-photon confocal fluorescence microscope with single excitation of 750 nm through a 63 × 1.4NA objective. The fluorescence emissions were collected at 385–495 nm (CD) and 500–650 nm (FA), respectively. Then, the fluorescence images were measured, and pH calibration curves was plotted using Leica Application Suite X, image analysis ImageJ, and OriginPro 2020.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scaffold 3D printing and cell seeding for cytoplasmic pH sensing in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cylindrical scaffolds (diameter: 6 mm; height: 6 mm) were designed to have a cubic internal architecture with 50% porosity and 300 µm pore size (Fig. 6A). To fabricate the scaffolds, a stereolithography printing technique was used [21]. 65 wt% Baghdadite particles (Peter Brehm; Germany) was first mixed with a photosensitive polymer using ball milling (Retsch PM 400, Germany) at 150 rpm for 3 h. The photosensitive polymer was 50% made of a commercial photopolymer (Formlabs clear resin, MI, US) and 50% of a dispersant (TWEEN® 20, Sigma Aldrich). The resulting ceramic resin was printed using a commercial printer (Form2 from Formlabs). After the printing, the scaffolds were washed with isopropyl alcohol to remove the excess resin. The samples were then heated at 2 °C/min to 600 °C and were kept at this temperature for 3 h to eliminate the organic component of the resin completely. The samples were then heated at the same rate to 1400 °C at which temperature the Baghdadite particles sintered for 3 h. Cooling the samples down to room temperature (25 °C) was done in 12 h, resulting in crack-free scaffold.

ASCs at passage 4 were also used for this experiment. Before cell seeding, the 3D printed Baghdadite scaffolds were sterilized by an autoclave. A suspension of 60 μL culture medium containing 50,000 ASCs was dropped gently onto scaffolds and incubated for 90 min at 37 °C to allow cells attachment, then the cell culture medium was added to culture the cells. After culturing overnight, the cell culture medium was removed and FACD suspended in the full media (800 µg/mL) were added to the scaffolds and incubated for 24 h. Next. the culture medium was removed, and the scaffolds were gently washed three times with PBS buffer. Finally, high K+ buffer containing 30 mM NaCl, 120 mM KCl, 1 mM CaCl2, 0.5 MgSO4, 1 mM NaH2PO4, 5 mM glucose, 20 mM HEPES, 15 µM nigericin with pH of 5 and immediately observed by two-photon confocal fluorescence microscope equipped with benchtop incubator (Leica TCS SP5 II) at 37℃ with 5% CO2 for 80 min.

Carbon dots were synthesized by our previously reported one-step hydrothermal method [8]. To obtain narrow particle size distribution and cut-off the fraction of unreacted ingredients or overreacted product, the CDs were separated according to size using dialysis, and the CDs with particle size distribution in the range of 30–100 kDa (henceforth referred to as 30–100 kDa-CD) were taken on to further studies [22]. We first evaluated the optical properties, surface chemistry and chemical functionality of 30–100 kDa-CDs. The absorption spectrum exhibited a main peak at 299 nm and lower energy shoulder peak at 336 nm (Fig. 1 A). The main absorption peak was attributed to the transition of delocalized electrons in the π-bonding electron cloud of C = C bonds to π*- antibonding electron bands. The shoulder peak in the absorption spectrum resulted from the transition of lone pair electrons from n-nonbonding electron bands to π*-antibonding electron bands [23,24]. The fluorescence excitation (FLE) spectrum exhibited a peak at ∼ 360 nm, corresponding to excitation energy traps on the surface of the 30–100 kDa-CD (Fig. 1A) [20]. Emission resulting from fluorescence excitation wavelength is likely to be due to electron relaxation in C = C and graphitic C-N bonds in the nitrogen doped graphitic sp2 hybridized cores (aromatic domain) and formation of sp3 hybridized carbon matrix surrounding core of CD, such as hydroxyl (O–H), carbonyl (C = O), carboxyl (COOH), epoxide (C-O-C), amine (NH2) and pyridine nitrogen (C = N) [24–26]. Furthermore, the 30–100 kDa-CDs demonstrated excitation dependency which is due to the presence of different functional groups with different band gap energy (Ebg) levels, which requires different λex and emits different λem (Fig. 1B) [20]. The presence of these characteristic bonds in the 30–100 kDa-CDs was confirmed by FT-IR and XPS techniques (Fig. 1C-D, and Figure S1). The XPS survey scan (Fig. 1D) of 30–100 kDa-CD displays three dominant peaks corresponding to C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (530 eV), indicating that there is no significant contaminant on the surface of the samples. The quantum yield and two-photon absorption cross-section was calculated to be ∼ 0.2 and ∼ 90 GM (Goeppert-Mayer unit, with 1 GM = 10–50 cm4s/photon) for these CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of a two-photon ratiometric ultrasensitive pH-responsive carbon dot-based nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: We assessed the pH-sensitivity of 30–100 kDa-CD in the range of 4–8 using fluorescence spectroscopy. The results showed minimal fluorescence changes with varying pH (Figure S2A), likely due to the presence of multiple protonatable functional groups with different pKa values (carboxyl - pKa ≈ 4, phenol - pKa ≈ 10, pyridinic - pKa ≈ 5.25 and pyrrolic nitrogen - pKa ≈ 7.5) [27]. The pH insensitivity of 30–100 kDa-CD means that it is appropriate for use as the reference component in the construction of ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for pH sensing. A screen of CD fluorescence response to different metal ions showed that only Fe3+ caused ∼ 50% quenching in fluorescence emission (Figure S2B). This can be attributed to the presence of negatively-charged hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the CD that exhibit a high affinity for Fe3+, which as a hard acid and according to hard-soft acid-base theory, will preferentially bind to these hard donor groups [8,20]. Cross-reactivity to metal ions is a significant challenge in the development of pH-sensitive nanoprobes [28–32]. Development of a nanoprobe with specified high specificity for pH changes is of a great importance, and it was therefore important in subsequent probe design to minimize the interaction of the CDs with Fe3+.

Having shown that the 30–100 kDa-CD themselves were insensitive to pH changes, we sought to decorated them with a pH sensitive moiety. Fluoresceinamine (5-aminofluorescein; FA) was selected due to its excellent pH sensitivity in the physiological pH range (4–8) both alone and when conjugated through its amine group (Figure S3) [33,34]. The pH response of FA towards pH variation is attributed to the protonation/deprotonation of its surface functional groups (hydroxyl), which directly affects their fluorescence intensity [35]. In this study, we designed a conjugation procedure in which the negatively charged functional groups of the CD (responsible for Fe3+ sensing) reacted with the amine group of FA. We anticipated that this would prevent interaction of Fe3+, and therefore minimize any Fe3+-induced quenching of CD fluorescence. The developed nanoprobe (hereafter referred to as FACD) was predicted to possess excellent sensitivity to pH and minimal response to metal ions and other biomolecules.

To verify the successful conjugation of FA to the CD, absorption and FT-IR spectra were collected. UV–vis spectroscopy revealed a sharp absorption peak at 495 nm, characteristic of fluorescein, with other absorption peaks at 275, 320, and 385 nm attributed to the 30–100 kDa-CD (Fig. 2 A). The presence of an additional peak at ∼ 528 nm in the fluorescence emission spectrum compared to the CD alone further confirmed the successful conjugation of FA to the CD. The FT-IR measurements confirmed successful conjugation of the FA due to disappearance of the FA amine functional groups at 1590 cm−1 and 30–100 kDa-CD hydroxyl/carboxyl functional groups at 1390 cm−1 after conjugation reaction (Figure S4). Two-photon fluorescence excitation and emission spectroscopy of FACD demonstrated two excitation peaks at 750 nm (CD) and 790 nm (FA) and two emission peaks at 445 nm (CD) and 525 nm (FA) using single excitation wavelength of 750 nm (Figure S5A). TEM image found the FACD particle size to be in the range of 5–10 nm (Fig. 2B).

To evaluate the pH sensing performance of our new nanoprobe, we monitored the emission intensity of FACD upon single-photon excitation at 350 nm in the presence of solutions with different pH (4–11), (Fig. 2C-D). The results showed a gradual increase in the emission intensity at 528 nm (corresponding to FA), whereas the emission intensity at 425 nm (corresponding to CD) remained largely constant by increasing pH from 4 to 11 (Fig. 2C). The fluorescence intensity ratios (IFA / ICD) reached plateau at pH 11 (Fig. 2D). Similar results were obtained upon two-photon excitation at 750 nm where the fluorescence emission spectra displayed two visible emission peaks at 442 nm (corresponding CD) and 528 nm (corresponding to FA), as shown in Figure S6A. The emission intensity at 528 nm gradually increased with the increase in pH from 5 to 7.5, however, we did not notice any change in the emission intensity at 442 nm. Similar to single-photon excitation, the ratio of two emission peak intensity (IFA / ICD) also showed an increase with pH, including the linearity in the range of pH from 6 to 7.5 (Figure S6B). The developed FACD is the first example of a two-photon excitable ratiometric CD-based fluorescent nanoprobe with excellent pH sensitivity.

Since the pH sensitivity of our nanoprobe falls within the physiological pH values of living cells (pH 4–8), FACD can be potentially used to accurately measure intracellular pH. To ensure the applicability of FACD for pH sensing in biological environments, the reversibility, specificity, and stability were further examined. We subjected the developed nanoprobe to pH cycling between 4 and 10 by sequentially adding acid or base to the solution containing FACD. The results showed a strong fluorescence reversibility of FACD nanoprobe even after 7 cycles (Figure S5B). FACD showed excellent thermal stability, with fluorescence intensity retained across the different pH values (4–10) at 25 to 37℃ (Fig. 3 A).

The photostability of the FACD was studied by exposing the nanoprobe to continuous UV light (500 W) irradiation for 1 h at different pHs (Fig. 3B). At all pH values, FACD exhibited no variations in fluorescence intensity over time. We then investigated the specificity of FACD towards metal ions and biomolecules as potential interferents for pH sensing in biological systems. The ratiometric fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe has no obvious changes in the presence of the potential interferents (Fig. 3C), confirming the excellent specificity of FACD toward pH. The insensitivity of the FACD to other competitive elements, particularly to Fe3+, can be attributed to the conjugation of the sensitive functional groups on the CD surface with FA.

We then examined the cytotoxicity of the FACD when incubated with adipose stem cells (ASCs) using an MTS assay. FACD showed excellent biocompatibility, as the cells did not show any significant changes in absorbance, even at the high concentration of 800 µg/mL after 7 d incubation (Fig. 3D). Live-cell imaging using two-photon confocal microscopy involved monitoring of two emission channels: 385–495 nm, corresponding to the CD itself, and 500–650 nm, corresponding to FA. Imaging study revealed that cells treated with 800 µg/mL FACD for 24 h at 37℃ and 5% CO2 displayed obvious signals from the two detector channels (385–495 nm and 500–650 nm) at excitation wavelength of 750 nm. These results suggest good internationalization of FACD by cells, and localization of nanoprobe to the cytoplasm (Figure S7). These imaging parameters were therefore employed for all subsequent studies.

Overall, in comparison to previous carbon dot-based nanoprobes, FACD illustrates superior properties (Table S1). In summary, our developed FACD nanoprobe gives a ratiometric response upon excitation at a single wavelength, is compatible with two-photon excitation, is selectively sensitive to pH changes, exhibits excellent thermal stability and photostability under different conditions, possesses superior biocompatibility and two-photon fluorescence live-cell imaging capability. These features make FACD an ideal candidate for biological applications and specifically for real-time quantifying and sensing dynamic changes of intracellular pH values in live cells [14,36]. We therefore sought to apply the nanoprobe to different biological questions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring of intracellular pH in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the potential of FACD for ratiometric measurement of intracellular pH value in living cells, we first incubated ASCs with FACD and then with high-K+ HEPES-buffered solution containing 10 µM nigericin with pH values ranging from 5.0 to 7.5. Nigericin is a polycyclic ether carboxylic acid compound that can regulate the movement of H+/K+, facilitating an equilibration of the intracellular pH and that of the culture medium [37]. The fluorescence intensity in channel 385–495 nm (CD fluorescence; red pseudocolor) remained stable and unaffected in pH range from 5.0 to 7.5 (Figure S8A). As expected, the blue fluorescence from channel 500–650 nm (FA fluorescence; blue pseudocolor) displayed weak fluorescence at a low cytosolic pH value of 5 (Figure S8B). The FA fluorescence intensity gradually increased with increasing pH from 5.0 to 7.5. The fluorescence ratio image (IFA/ICD) was obtained using the RatioPlus Plugin ImageJ. A significant color change in the fluorescence ratio images was observed with increasing pH (third row in Fig. 4 A). The corresponding intracellular fluorescence intensity of FACD for each sample were quantified using image analysis software (ImageJ 1.53a) and the ratiometric data are plotted in Fig. 4B. Results indicate a significant and linear change in the fluorescence intensity ratio obtained from cells as pH values increase from 5.0 to 7.5 (Fig. 4B; r2 = 0.9763.). This could enable the accurate quantification of pH changes in living cells.

To evaluate FACD’s capability to monitor the dynamic process of pH changes in live cells, we performed time-course two-photon fluorescence imaging of ASCs incubated with FACD and treated with K+ buffer (1 mM, pH 5.0 containing 10 µM nigericin). Cells were imaged at 5 min intervals for 40 min after the treatment. The results revealed no change in the intensity of the 385–495 channel consistent with the insensitivity of CD fluorescence to pH change (Fig. 5 A and S8B). In contrast, the fluorescence in the 500–650 nm channel, corresponding to FA emission, gradually decreased, and almost quenched after 40 min (Figure S8B). The fluorescence ratio images (last two rows of Fig. 5A) show a significant change in the color after 25 min of treatment. The ratio (IFA / ICD) consistently decreased over the incubation period (Fig. 5B). This reduction in the IFA / ICD ratio reflected the high rate of K+/H+ exchange due to the enhanced effect of nigericin on the regulation of the electrochemical proton gradient. These results are consistent with the reported role of nigericin as an antiporter in the regulation of intracellular pH value as well as the capability of the FACD for accurate dynamic monitoring and quantifying of pH variations in the live cells [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring of intracellular pH in live cells seeded on 3D environment
ce:section.ce:para: In the human body, cells live in a three-dimensional (3D) environment known as the extracellular matrix (ECM) that give cells structural support and is involved in biochemical signaling. More efforts are now directed to studying cell behavior in a more represented 3D in vitro cultures, as opposed to two-dimensional (2D) in vitro cultures, which disregard the complexity of cell-3D ECM interactions in vivo. It is therefore important to be able to monitor intracellular pH variation in cells grown in 3D rather than 2D environments. In this study, we capitalized on our expertise in developing novel engineered 3D printed ceramic scaffolds mimicking bone’s 3D ECM to monitor the intracellular pH in ASCs seeded on 3D environment in real time. We choose a 3D printed bioactive Baghdadite ceramic scaffold (Ca3ZrSi2O9); a calcium silicate- based ceramic where we introduced zirconium into the crystal structure of calcium silicate and demonstrated their outstanding in vitro and in vivo bioactivity [39].

We seeded ASCs into a 3D printed cylindrical Baghdadite scaffold with a cubic internal architecture (50% porosity and 300 µm pore size, Fig. 6 A). We then incubated these scaffolds with FACD for 24 h. The cell attachment and distribution on the surface of the scaffold as well as the effective cellular uptake of FACD by seeded ASCs can be seen in Fig. 6B. The cellular attachment and uptake of FACD can be further seen in the z-stack images collected from the surface of the scaffold to 120 μm; Δz = 10 μm (Fig. 6C-O).

ASCs seeded in the scaffold and incubated with FACD were exposed to highly concentrated K+ buffer with pH of 5 containing 10 µM nigericin. We immediately performed time-course two-photon fluorescence imaging of the ASCs to study the potential for real-time monitoring of pH changes in live ASCs seeded on the scaffold. The variation in the intracellular fluorescence intensity of FACD with changing extracellular pH (Fig. 7 A) shows a very similar behavior to the ASC monolayer (Fig. 5A). The fluorescence originating from the CD (385–495 nm channel) remained unchanged and the fluorescence arising from FA (500–650 nm channel) gradually decreased with fluorescence intensity completely diminishing after 80 min of treatment (Figure S8C). The ratio plot (Fig. 7B) shows that the pH equilibration of ASCs on the Baghdadite scaffold is about two-fold slower than for monolayer ASCs (Fig. 5B). This delay could be related to attachment and proliferation of the ASCs on the surface of the scaffold which might reduce the diffusion surface area and consequently the rate of ion diffusion (K+/H+) into the cells. These results indicate that FACD can be utilized for multifunctional purposes as (i) two-photon fluorescence imaging agent for monitoring the live cells while seeded on the synthetic scaffolds (ii) two-photon ratiometric nanoprobe for real-time visualizing and quantifying cytoplasmic pH values in living cells both in vitro and in vivo.

In summary, we have successfully developed the first two-photon ratiometric carbon dot-based fluorescent nanoprobe (FACD) with an ability for precise real-time monitoring and quantification of the intracellular pH values in both 2D and 3D environments. Our findings suggest that FACD has a superb ratiometric response to pH changes in the range of 4–10. The developed nanoprobe demonstrates an excellent biocompatibility of ∼ 100% after 7 days of treatment, remarkable thermalstability (up to 37℃) and photostability (3600 s), and two-photon near-infrared excitation capability (excitation wavelength of 750 nm). We showed that FACD can be utilized as a nanoprobe for accurate long-term and real-time visualizing and quantifying of cytoplasmic pH dynamic fluctuations mediated by nigericin in both 2D and 3D environments. Therefore, these results indicate the significant potential of the developed FACD nanoprobe for ultrasensitive sensing of pH value in living cells both in vitro and in vivo. Furthermore, this study provides a new avenue for the future developments of ratiometric carbon-based nanoprobe for targeting a variety of other analytes of biological interest by conveniently conjugating the appropriate fluorophores and probes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the following funding sources: The Australian Research Council (Grant No. IC170100022), The National Health and Medical Council (Grant No, APP1107470), The Australian centre for Microscopy and Microanalysis facilities for transmission electron microscopy, and two-photon confocal microscopy, The Sydney Analytical for spectroscopic measurements. Authors PL wish to thank The University of Sydney for the scholarship support.

The experiments were designed and conceived by P.L and H.Z. The carbon dot synthesis, characterization was conducted by P.L and M.M. The nanoprobe development, characterization, live cell imaging, intracellular sensing and imaging were performed by P.L. Cell culture was performed by Z.L. 3D bioprinting of scaffold was conducted by M.Mirkhalaf. The manuscript was written by P.L and revised by G.S. E.J.N., and H.Z.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.133668.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
